coredata.dc:title: Supramolecular self-assembly of carbon nanodots through edge functionalized interaction
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Cheng-Long
Lou, Qing
Liu, Kai-Kai
Zheng, Guang-Song
Song, Run-Wei
Zang, Jin-Hao
Dong, Lin
Shan, Chong-Xin
coredata.dc:description: Control of aggregation plays an important role in the photophysical behavior of luminescent nanomaterials. Herein, the concentration-dependent self-assembled hybrid of carbon nanodots (CDs) with the alteration spectral characters of H-/J-aggregation were observed. In detail, the CDs aging under different conditions presented obvious spectral variations ranging from visible to near-infrared regions, accompanied by the spontaneous evolution of nanoaggregates from CD nanoparticles. Theoretical calculations and experimental results confirmed the inhomogeneous surface functional groups could render CDs into nanoaggregates with the H- or J-aggregation through intraparticle or interparticle interaction, inducing the discrepant surface-confined charges in these intraparticle nanoaggregates and further contributing to their shift of absorption and emission bands. With the novel aggregation regulation, the CDs exhibited a tunable increase in their photothermal and photodynamic capability in comparison to their original counterparts. These findings shed a new light on the understanding of the photophysical behaviors for the self-assembled hybrid of CDs with the concept of supramolecular engineering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Self-assembled hybrid
Supramolecular engineering
Photophysical properties
Photothermal conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs
Photochemical free radicals and single oxygen determination
Synthesis of the original and assembled CDs
Optical characterizations of the assembled CDs
Structure characterizations of the assembled CDs
Possible mechanism of the assembled CDs
Promising applications of the assembled CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM and HRTEM images of o-CDs and the SAD pattern of HRTEM image. (b) The AFM image of o-CDs (inset: the height of the monodispersed nanoparticles). (c) The full survey XPS spectrum of o-CDs (inset: C1s spectrum of o-CDs). (d) The excitation-emission of freshly obtained o-CDs aqueous solution (300 mg mL−1). (e) The excitation-emission matrix of diluted o-CDs solution (∼0.6 mg mL−1). (f) The UV–vis absorption for 0, 16, 64 and 512-multiple dilution of 300 mg mL−1 o-CDs solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The excitation-emission of 30 mg mL−1 b-CDs solution. (b) The excitation-emission of 0.6 mg mL−1 b-CDs solution. (c) The PL emission of 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs and 30/0.6 mg mL−1 b-CDs solution. (d) The UV–vis absorption of 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64 and 128-multiple diluted 30 mg mL−1 b-CDs solution. (e) The excitation-emission of 30 mg mL−1 gr-CDs solution. (f) The excitation-emission of 0.6 mg mL−1 gr-CDs solution. (g) The PL emission of 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs and 30/0.6 mg mL−1 gr-CDs solution. (h) The UV–vis absorption of 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, and 512-multiple diluted 30 mg mL−1 gr-CDs solution. (i) The excitation-emission of 30 mg mL−1 g-CDs solution. (j) The excitation-emission of 0.6 mg mL−1 g-CDs solution. (k) The PL emission of 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs and 30/0.6 mg mL−1 g-CDs solution. (l) The UV–vis absorption of 8, 16, 32, 64 and 128-multiple diluted 30 mg mL−1 g-CDs solution. (m) The excitation-emission of 30 mg mL−1 r-CDs solution. (n) The excitation-emission of 0.6 mg mL−1 r-CDs solution. (o) The PL emission of 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs and 30/0.6 mg mL−1 r-CDs solution. (p) The UV–vis absorption of 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64 and 128-multiple diluted 30 mg mL−1 r-CDs solution.

(a–d) The TEM images of b-CDs (a), gr-CDs (b), g-CDs (c) and r-CDs (d). (e–h) The HRTEM images and corresponding SAD pattern of the obtained b-CDs (e), gr-CDs (f), g-CDs (g), and r-CDs (h). (i–l) The AFM images of the obtained b-CDs (i), gr-CDs (j), g-CDs (k), and r-CDs (l). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The XPS spectra of full-survey (a), C1s (b) N1s (c) and O1s (d) for b-CDs. (e–h) The XPS spectra of full-survey (e), C1s (f) N1s (g) and O1s (h) for gr-CDs. (i–l) The XPS spectra of full-survey (i), C1s (j), N1s (k) and O1s (l) for g-CDs. (m–p) The XPS spectra of full-survey (m), C1s (n) N1s (o) and O1s (p) for r-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic illustration of the AF-GNS, CF-GNS and ACF-GNS. (b) Schematic illustration of the approaches to the aggregates from the GNS, ACF-GNS and ACF-N-GNS. (c) The corresponding HOMO and LUMO levels, ground state dipole moment (ug) and excited state dipole moment (ueg) from the charge density distributions of GNS, ACF-GNS and ACF-N-GNS. (d) Schematic representations of the structure, energy alignment and absorption principle of intraparticler H-couping, interparticle H-couping, intraparticle J-couping and interparticle J-couping for the b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The powder of the b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs stored for three months. (b) The excitation-emission matrix for dispersed b-CDs aqueous solution. (c) The excitation-emission matrix for dispersed g-CDs aqueous solution. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra for the dispersed b-CDs and g-CDs aqueous solution. (e) The temperature of pure water, 100 mg mL−1 b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs, or r-CDs aqueous solution under the irradiation of 780 nm laser. (f) The temperature of 25, 50 and 100 mg L−1 b-CDs aqueous solution with the irradiation of 780 nm laser on and off. (g) The ESR spectra upon irradiation of b-CDs solution for detecting different ROS species of 1O2 and •OH. (h) The ESR spectra upon irradiation of g-CDs solution for detecting different ROS species of 1O2 and •OH. (i) The proposed mechanism for the interaction between water, dissolved oxygen and b-CDs under light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Supramolecular self-assembly of carbon nanodots through edge functionalized interaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, Key Laboratory of Material Physics, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450052, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, Key Laboratory of Material Physics, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450052, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Control of aggregation plays an important role in the photophysical behavior of luminescent nanomaterials. Herein, the concentration-dependent self-assembled hybrid of carbon nanodots (CDs) with the alteration spectral characters of H-/J-aggregation were observed. In detail, the CDs aging under different conditions presented obvious spectral variations ranging from visible to near-infrared regions, accompanied by the spontaneous evolution of nanoaggregates from CD nanoparticles. Theoretical calculations and experimental results confirmed the inhomogeneous surface functional groups could render CDs into nanoaggregates with the H- or J-aggregation through intraparticle or interparticle interaction, inducing the discrepant surface-confined charges in these intraparticle nanoaggregates and further contributing to their shift of absorption and emission bands. With the novel aggregation regulation, the CDs exhibited a tunable increase in their photothermal and photodynamic capability in comparison to their original counterparts. These findings shed a new light on the understanding of the photophysical behaviors for the self-assembled hybrid of CDs with the concept of supramolecular engineering.

Aggregation plays an important role in the photophysical behavior of luminescent nanomaterials. Herein, the concentration-dependent self-assembled hybrid of carbon nanodots (CDs) with the alteration spectral characters of H-/J-aggregation was observed. Theoretical calculations and experimental results confirmed the inhomogeneous surface functional groups could render CDs into nanoaggregates with different aggregation states such as H- and J-aggregation through intraparticle or interparticle interaction, inducing the discrepant surface-confined charges in these intraparticle nanoaggregates and further contributing to their shift of absorption and emission bands. This work presents a novel concept of supramolecular engineering in CDs, which can be used to understand the luminescence mechanism and simultaneously regulate the photophysical properties of CDs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) have recently been an emerging star member in the family of carbon nanomaterials [1–3]. Due to their distinctive photophysical properties such as tunable emission wavelength, high photoluminescence quantum yield (PL QY), excellent photostability, etc., various CDs have been employed in light-emitting devices (LEDs), photocatalysis, bioimaging, biosensors and photoactive therapy [4–12]. More recently, the CDs-based aggregates exhibit more attractive photophysical properties than their monodispersed particles [3,13–16]. For example, Li et al. constructed a novel type of supra-CDs by the assembly of surface charge-confined CDs through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, and Jiang's group discussed the mechanism of CD-based aggregation-induced room-temperature phosphorescence [14,17,18]. These CDs-based aggregates exhibit obvious alteration in basic and recognizable spectral properties, providing a new pathway to manipulate the spectral properties of CDs. Therefore, there still exist many challenges to understand and further manipulate the aggregation of CDs.

The supramolecular chemistry about the relationship between the characters of molecules and their aggregation states have been investigated for many years [19–23]. In detail, there are several concepts about the photophysical properties of molecules and their aggregates [24–27]. For examples, the J-aggregates of dye molecular illustrate a red-shift in absorption band compared with their monomers and a nearly resonant fluorescence with narrow band. In contrast, the H-aggregation of molecules exhibit blueshift in absorption and low or no fluorescence with respect to their monomers [27–30]. Generally, the H-aggregated molecules align face-to-face while the H-aggregated molecules connected head to tail. Thus, the dipoles of H-aggregated molecules repel each other and hold in the high-energy state while the dipoles of the H-aggregated molecules attract each other and are in the low energy state. Therefore, the low energy state transition is forbidden in H aggregation while the high energy state transition in J aggregation is forbidden, leading to the blueshift and redshift [24–27]. These basic and recognizable spectral properties of the molecular are significantly altered in their building blocks [31,32]. Meanwhile, more sophisticated studies on the electronic properties of J-aggregates reveals that excited states can be formed by extended domains of coherently coupled molecular transition dipoles, advocating for J-aggregation as archetype phenomenon for an emerging systems property on the supramolecular or nanoscale level [33–35]. For the CDs, there have been some relevant reports on the possible J-aggregations in the CDs-based nanoaggregates. For instance, Qu et al. has achieved white emissive CDs actuated by the H-/J-aggregates and Förster resonance energy transfer [36]. However, the pathway from CDs to CD-based nanoaggregates is far from clear. Hence, it is significant to employ the supramolecular chemistry to bring an insight into the principle of CDs aggregation and corresponding aggregation-induced photophysical characters.

In this work, the concentration-dependent self-assembled hybrid was observed in CDs aqueous solution, and the supramolecular chemistry was employed to understand their alterations of spectral characters. In details, the CDs kept in different concentration showed obvious changes in their absorption and emission spectral characters with different nanoaggregates gradually. With the experimental characterizations and first-principles theoretical calculations based on the density functional theory (DFT), the self-assembled hybrids with intraparticle and interparticle interaction were deduced to spontaneously occur in these CDs aqueous solution, resulting in their changes in spectral properties. Moreover, the inhomogeneous surface functional groups could promote CDs to form different aggregation states, like H- and J-aggregation, through the inconsistent surface-confined charges in the corresponding intraparticle nanoaggregates, leading to their near-infrared (NIR) absorption band and PL shift. These CD-based nanoaggregates exhibited adjustable photothermal and photodynamic properties, enabling their promising applications in photothermal and photodynamic therapy. These results provide new insights into the mechanism about the alterations of photophysical behaviors for the self-assembled hybrid of CDs through supramolecular engineering.

Citric acid (C6H8O7, CA, 99.9%) and urea (CON2H4, 99.9%) were purchased by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, P. R. China). Ethyl alcohol (C2H6O, EtOH, 99.9%), acetone (C3H6O, Ace, 99.9%), Dimethylacetamide (C4H9NO, DMAC, 99.9%) and N, N-Dimethylformamide (C3H7NO, DMF, 99.9%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, P. R. China).

The surface morphology was characterized by a field emission transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JSM-IT100) by depositing the corresponding CDs drops on the carbon-coated copper grids and atomic force microscopy (AFM, AIST-NT Smart SPM) by depositing the o-CDs on silicon. The X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical X’ Pert Pro) using Cu kα as the irradiation source was used to obtain the XRD patterns. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded on a Bio-Rad Excalibur spectrometer (Bruker vector 22). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on a Kratos AXIS HIS 165 spectrometer with a monochromatized Al KR X-ray source (1486.7 eV). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum was collected using a Shimadzu UV-3101PC spectrometer. The fluorescence excitation-emission matrixes were measure with a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. The Zeta potential distributions were measured with Zetasizer Nano.

The original CDs (o-CDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal procedure as previously reported. Actually, citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) were used as precursors to dissolve in 10 ml water. Then the mixed solution was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (20 mL) and placed into an electric oven. After treated at 160 °C for 4 h, the freshly obtained solution kept in Teflon-lined stainless autoclave was frozen without air in refrigerator and then freeze-dried by vacuum freeze dryer. The obtained white CDs solids were further characterized as o-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the b-CDs, when the white o-CDs powder was kept in air, the white powder gradually becomes black within one day. The as-obtained black powder can be dispersed in water to form blue-color solution (b-CDs, 300 mg mL−1). For the gr-CDs, the fresh 10 mL o-CDs aqueous solution (300 mg mL−1) were concentrated to 1 mL by rotary evaporator and the as-obtained o-CDs solution (3 g mL−1) were kept in air. The fresh obtained solution gradually changed from yellow to entirely grey within three days. Then 1 mL obtained solution was dispersed in 100 mL water to form grey-color aqueous solution (gr-CDs, 30 mg mL−1). For the g-CDs, the 10 mL fresh o-CDs solution (300 mg mL−1) was directly kept in air and the clear solution gradually changed from clear to green-color within three days. Then 1 mL obtained solution was dispersed in 10 mL water to form green-color aqueous solution (g-CDs, 30 mg mL−1). For the r-CDs, the 1 mL freshly o-CDs solution (300 mg mL−1) was dispersed into 100 mL water and the clear o-CDs solution (3 mg mL−1) gradually changed from clear to red within three months. Then, the obtained CDs solution was concentrated and redispersed into 10 mL water to obtained red-color aqueous solution (r-CDs, 30 mg mL−1) by rotary evaporator. All these aqueous solutions were further frozen and freeze-dried by vacuum freeze dryer for further characterizations.

All the calculations were performed on Gaussian 16 program. The stable structures were optimized by using PBE0 with the basic set of 6-31G(d). The dipole moments were further calculated with def2-SVPD. The D3(BJ) empirical dispersion correction was considered to make the results more accurate.

To examine the photothermal behaviors of different CDs in solution, these CDs solution were irradiated with 780 nm NIR light. Briefly, 100 mg mL−1 o-CDs, b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs aqueous solutions mixed with 4 mL deionized water were deposited into the Eppendorf tube, respectively. The tubes containing different CD solution were irradiated with a 780 nm continuous-wave laser at a power density of 10 W cm−2, respectively. The temperatures were recorded using an infrared thermal imaging camera. Meanwhile, the aqueous solution containing the b-CDs with different concentrations (100, 50, and 25 mg mL−1) under the continuous irradiation and long exposed time (30 s) with laser on and off were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photochemical free radicals and single oxygen determination
ce:section.ce:para: In photocatalytic reactions, electrons and holes generated from photocatalyst reacting with O2 and H2O to form ROS, such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2). Using TEMPO (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl) and TEMP (2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidine) as spin probes, the content of 1O2, and •OH could be detected by electron paramagnetic resonance (ESR). The main active species including 1O2, and •OH could be produced by the b-CDs and g-CDs under the light irradiation of 650 nm laser for 5 min.

For living cell staining experiments, the b-CDs were dispersed in Phosphate Buffer (PBS) with the concertation from 0 to 500 μg mL−1. And a laboratory-built chamber with different concentrations of b-CDs dispersing in culture medium was used to culture the HeLa cells at 37 °C. After 12 h, the cell viability was measured with the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the original and assembled CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Citric acid and urea are employed as precursors to prepare the original CDs (o-CDs) via the solvothermal strategy as previous reports [14,36,37]. With the transmission electron microscope (TEM) by depositing the fresh o-CDs aqueous solution on copper and atomic force microscopy (AFM) by depositing the o-CDs on silicon, the morphologies of o-CDs are investigated. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the TEM image of o-CDs illustrates obvious nanoparticles with diameters around 5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the single nanoparticle exhibits the circular shape with clearly visible lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon. In the AFM image, the o-CDs illustrate the heights ranging from 0.5 to 5 nm (Fig. 1b), which are corresponding to their TEM image. To further confirm the crystal phase of o-CDs, the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) has been investigated and a broad peak corresponding to the (100) crystal plane located at 21° can be observed (Fig. S1), which is similar to the previous reports. In addition, the chemical elements and surface groups of the o-CDs are investigated by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 1c, the o-CDs exhibit the element of carbon (∼246 eV), nitrogen (∼399 eV) and oxygen (∼532 eV) in full-survey XPS spectrum (Fig. S2) and the C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three Gaussian peaks corresponding to CC/C–C, C–C/C–N, CO and –C(O)–O. Meanwhile, the N1s spectrum of o-CDs can be deconvoluted as amino N and graphitic N (Fig. S2) and their O1s spectrum are quinone O and carbonyl O (Fig. S3). The FT-IR spectrum of o-CDs exhibits the characteristic absorption bands of ν (O–H)/ν (N–H) (3100–3500 cm−1), ν (C
O)/δ(N–H)/ν(C
N) (1550–1750 cm−1) and δ(C–H)/ν (C–N) (1330–1480 cm−1) (Fig. S4). Combined with the survey XPS and FT-IR spectra, the existence of functional groups, such as amino, carboxyl and aromatic CN heterocycles can be confirmed in the initial o-CDs. In addition, the excitation-emission matrix of freshly o-CDs has been further investigated in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 1d, the o-CD aqueous solution (300 mg mL−1) exhibits an excitation-dependent emission with a center at (495 nm, 590 nm). The CDs after treated with thermal or ultrasound treatment exhibit the similar excitation-dependent PL emission with the maximum excitation-emission center (Fig. S5), implying their well stability of the concentration-dependent emission. Interesting, when the freshly obtained o-CDs solution is diluted with water, the obtained clear o-CD solution (∼0.6 mg mL−1) exhibits an excitation-independent emission with three centers at (260 nm, 435 nm), (295 nm, 435 nm) and (385 nm, 435 nm) (Fig. 1e). In consequence, the fresh o-CDs and its diluted solution present obvious different emission spectra under their corresponding maximal excitation wavelength (Fig. 1f). In these excitation-emission matrixes, the excitation-emission center gradually disappear with the dilution (Fig. S6). These gradual alterations of PL excitation-emission directly confirm the possible weak interparticle interaction between CD nanoparticles in aqueous solution. Similarly, the fresh o-CDs and their diluted solutions illustrate different UV–vis absorption. As shown in Fig. 1g, the fresh o-CDs solution exhibits continuous absorption in visible light region while its diluted aqueous solution illustrates one special absorption band in ultraviolet area (<300 nm), which is always considered as the π–π* transition of sp2 C domain in sp3 matrix. Moreover, the initial CDs aqueous solutions prepared with different mass ratio of citric acid and urea exhibit the similar PL excitation-emission (Fig. S7), confirming that the photophysical properties of the formed CDs are independent on the concentration of reaction source and the higher concentration of o-CDs solution can't provide more redshift. The concentration-dependent excitation-emission matrix and UV–vis absorption spectra imply that the potential interaction between different CD particles in aqueous environment. When the fresh o-CDs are kept in different aqueous environment aging for several days, the blue, grey, green and red aqueous solutions named as b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs solution can be gradually formed as illustrated in Experiments section. Meanwhile, the PL excitation-emission of the solutions obtained by mixing 1 mL o-CDs aqueous solution with 1 mL Ace, EtOH, DMAC and DMF illustrate little alteration as the initial o-CDs aqueous solution (Fig. S8), confirming that the alteration isn't from the solvent effect and the organic solvent can't promote the alteration. Compared with the o-CDs, the obtained b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs aqueous solutions illustrate obviously different absorption and PL emission behaviors (Fig. 1h and i), implying their different spectral characters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterizations of the assembled CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Whereupon, the spectral characters of these solution are further investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 a, 30 mg mL−1 b-CDs aqueous solution exhibits ultraweak PL emission under the excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 600 nm. Meanwhile, when the b-CDs is diluted to 0.6 mg mL−1, the obtained clear aqueous solution still illustrates quite weak fluorescence (Fig. 2b). In their corresponding excitation-emission matrix, an excitation-dependent center around (385 nm, 425 nm) can be observed, which is as similar as 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs aqueous solution. Meanwhile, compared with the initial 300 mg mL−1 o-CDs aqueous solution, the PL intensity of 30 mg mL−1 b-CDs solution is far lower than that of 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs aqueous solution (Fig. 2c). However, although 0.6 mg mL−1 b-CDs aqueous solution illustrates a similar PL spectrum profile as the 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs, its PL intensity is still far lower than 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs aqueous solution (Fig. 2c). Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectra for the different concentrations of b-CDs aqueous solution have been investigated. As shown in Fig. 2d, these b-CDs aqueous solutions illustrate a well-defined strong absorption band around 650 nm, covering the completely visible and NIR spectral ranging from 470 to 800 nm. When the 30 mg mL−1 b-CDs solution is diluted, the obtained clear solution illustrates stable UV–vis absorption bands around 650 nm, endowing distinct difference from the initial o-CD aqueous solution. As similar as the b-CDs, the gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs aqueous solution are investigated with the same characterizations. As shown in Fig. 2e and f, the 30 mg mL−1 gr-CDs aqueous solution exhibits ultraweak PL emission as similar 30 mg mL−1 b-CDs while its diluted solution (0.6 mg mL−1) exhibits quite strong PL emission under the excitation wavelength ranging from 250 nm to 550 nm. Similarly, 30 and 0.6 mg mL−1 gr-CDs aqueous solution exhibits lower PL intensity than 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs solution (Fig. 2g), and the UV–vis absorption spectra for different concentrations of gr-CDs aqueous solution illustrate a continues absorption in the UV to NIR area ranging from 200 nm to 800 nm (Fig. 2h). Compared with the o-CDs, the obtained g-CDs and r-CDs aqueous solutions exhibit more differences in PL emission and UV–vis absorption. As shown in Fig. 2i, 30 mg mL−1 g-CDs aqueous solution exhibits bright PL emission under the excitation wavelength ranging from 380 to 580 nm with an excitation-dependent center at (495 nm, 590 nm) as similar as the o-CDs. However, the g-CDs diluted solution (0.6 mg mL−1) exhibits intense PL emission under different excitation wavelength with one excitation-dependent center at (295 nm, 435 nm) and another excitation-dependent center at (385 nm, 435 nm) (Fig. 2j). Compared with 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs aqueous solution, 30 and 0.6 mg mL−1 g-CDs aqueous solutions still exhibit lower PL intensity under their maximal excitation wavelength (Fig. 2k). Meanwhile, the UV–vis absorption for different concentrations of g-CDs aqueous solution illustrate the well-defined strong absorption bands around 650 nm (Fig. 2l) as similar as the b-CDs aqueous solution. What's more, the obtained 30 mg mL−1 r-CDs aqueous solution exhibits intense PL emission under different excitation wavelength with two excitation-dependent centers around (295 nm, 435 nm) and (385 nm, 435 nm), and another excitation-independent center around (495 nm, 590 nm) (Fig. 2m). However, the r-CDs diluted solution (0.6 mg mL−1) exhibits different excitation-emission matrix with only one excitation-independent center around (360 nm, 435 nm) (Fig. 2n). Meanwhile, the 30 and 0.6 mg mL−1 r-CDs aqueous solutions exhibit similar PL emission and PL intensity under their maximal excitation wavelength as 0.6 mg mL−1 o-CDs aqueous solution (Fig. 2o). In addition, the 30 mg mL−1 r-CDs aqueous solution illustrates a strong absorption in the UV to NIR spectral ranging from 200 nm to 800 nm and the strong absorption in visible area changes when the r-CDs aqueous solution is diluted (Fig. 2p), as similar as the initial o-CDs. All these surveys confirm the different spectral characters of these CDs-based products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterizations of the assembled CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insight into the structural features of b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs, their microscopy images are conducted. As shown in Fig. 3 a–d, different nanoaggregates with larger sizes and inhomogeneous structures can be observed in the TEM images of b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs, indicating the possible assembly of monodispersed CDs. In the TEM images, the b-CDs and gr-CDs exhibit small cluster containing several CD nanoparticles (Fig. 3e and f). However, the g-CDs and r-CDs illustrate quite disorder conglomerations, which contain abundant CD nanoparticles (Fig. 3g and h). Meanwhile, the HRTEM images reveal the presence of a crystalline graphite phase in all these CDs-based nanoaggregates, indicating the same original carbon nanoparticles from the o-CDs. In the AFM images (Fig. 3i and j), the heights of b-CDs and gr-CDs illustrate homogeneous distribution ranging from 1 nm to 20 nm. However, the g-CDs and r-CDs exhibit increased heights in the range of 20∼200 nm (Fig. 3k and l), corresponding to the results of TEM images. On the condition, it can be deduced that there are different nanoaggregates formed by the assembled monodispersed CD nanoparticles in the b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs aqueous solution.

To further probe the pathway to form the nanoaggregates in the b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs aqueous solution derived from monodispersed o-CDs, the elements and chemical constituents of the obtained o-CDs, b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs are determined by the XPS. As shown in Fig. 4 a, e, 4i, and 4m, all these CDs powders exhibit the elements of carbon (∼246 eV), nitrogen (∼399 eV) and oxygen (∼532 eV) as same as the o-CDs. Meanwhile, the C1s spectra of these CDs powders are detected as CC/C–C, C–C/C–N, CO and –C(O)–O (Fig. 4b, f, 4j and 4n), the N1s spectra are considered as amino N and graphitic N (Fig. 4c, g, 4k and 4o) and the O1s spectra are quinone O and carbonyl O (Fig. 4d, h, 4l, and 4p), confirming the similar element structure of different products. However, the chemical constituents of b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs powders illustrate different ratio. It can be found that the components of CO in the b-CDs, g-CDs, gr-CDs and r-CDs powders are different from the o-CDs, implying the connection between the surface groups of different o-CDs. In addition, the ratio of amino N and graphitic N and the ratio of quinone O and carbonyl O for the b-CDs, gr-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs oxygen are also different from the o-CDs, further confirming the possible complex interaction existed in these CD-based nanoaggregates. Moreover, the b-CDs and gr-CDs aqueous solutions illustrate obvious different Zeta potential from the o-CDs solution while the g-CDs and r-CDs aqueous solutions illustrate similar Zeta potential from the o-CDs solution (Fig. S9). The different Zeta potentials between the o-CDs solution and b-CDs, gr-CDs imply the possible electrostatic interaction in these CDs-based nanoaggregates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the assembled CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to clarify the interaction between the assembled CD-based nanoaggregates, the theoretical calculations based on the DFT theory are carried out. Considering the XPS results and practical precursor of citric acid and urea for the preparation of CDs, the nanoparticles with amino groups, carboxyl groups, or both amino groups and carboxyl groups can be form in the solvothermal process as shown in Fig. 5 a. On the condition, the defined single-sheet graphene geometries known as graphene nanosheet (GNS), amino/carboxyl functional graphene nanosheet (ACF-GNS) and amino/carboxyl functional N-doped graphene nanosheet (ACF-N-GNS) are established to investigate the possible mechanism of CD-aggregation. Based on the probable built-up mode, different aggregates can be formed through the interparticle or intraparticle interaction as illustrated in Fig. 5b. When there are only GNS, they can spontaneously form the interparticle H-coupling through the interaction of π-π stacking. However, when there are both ACF-GNS or ACF-N-CNG in the water, these nanosheets can spontaneously form the interparticle H-coupling through the interaction of π-π stacking or J-coupling through the electronic interaction. Moreover, the interparticle H-coupling and J-coupling can be transform to the intraparticle H-coupling and J-coupling driven by the possible electrostatic interactions between the amino groups and carboxyl groups. With the D3(BJ) empirical dispersion correction, the incorporation of positive/negative surface groups of different graphene geometries in water and their corresponding distribution of energy level are calculated. As shown in Fig. 5c, these graphene geometries exhibit different ground state dipole moment (μg) and excited state dipole moment (μeg) for GNS, ACF-CNG and ACF-N-GNS. In these geometries, the GNS presents zero dipole moment in ground state and excited state due to their smooth charge distribution. Compared with the GNS, the ACF-CNG and ACF-N-GNS illustrate a polarization with a ground state dipole moment of 46.9 D and 38.8 D, and an excited state dipole moment of 43.8 D and 37.6 D under the ionization of water. All these calculated results confirm the self-assembled hybrid behavior of these CD nanoparticles in water. On the condition, the energy level distributions of GNS, ACF-CNG and ACF-N-GNS are further calculated. As shown in Fig. 5c, all these geometries illustrate a two-level schematic picture with an energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO, implying the possible PL emission of monodispersed CDs. In detail, the GNS illustrates one a two-level schematic picture with the HOMO level of −5.27 eV, LUMO level of −2.06 eV and an energy gap of 3.21 eV. Compared with the simple GNS, the ACF-GNS and ACF-N-GNS illustrate barely changed energy gap by evoking additional trap-state energy levels defined as another a level from amino groups and d level for carboxyl groups. This another energy decreases the band gap of CNS, implying the red-shift absorption and PL emission for the CDs with the geometries of ACF-GNS and ACF-N-GNS. Furthermore, the model of interparticle and intraparticle H-/J-aggregation are established to elucidate the changes of spectral characters in the nanoaggregates of b-CDs, gr-CDs g-CDs and r-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5d, these monodispersed nanoparticles can self-assemble to nanoaggregates in aqueous solution due to their polarization and charge. In these models, the possible intraparticle H-aggregates can be employed to explain the decreased PL and well-defined strong absorption bands around 650 nm for the b-CDs. Due to the π-π stacking, the H-aggregation directly decreases the PL emission and the electron transition between different nanoparticles results in the special well-defined strong absorption bands around 650 nm. In the monodispersed nanoparticles, the electron transition from a level to d level is almost forbidden because of the spatial separation of their charge density distributions. However, when different nanoparticles form the intraparticle nanoaggregates, the respective a and d levels in different nanoparticles can overlap spatially, resulting in the enhanced visible-NIR absorption. Whereas, the possible interparticle H-aggregation can be used to explain the decreased PL and changed UV–vis absorption without the well-defined strong absorption bands for the gr-CDs. Considering the disordered H-aggregation without electric interaction, the gr-CDs can exhibit similar ultraweak PL emission and different absorbance. In the nanoaggregates of gr-CDs, the spatial arrangements of adjacent nanoparticles are restricted, leading to the electron transitions from the a to d levels unfavorable and ineffective compared with the b-CDs. Moreover, the diverse PL emission and well-defined strong absorption bands in the g-CDs are deduced from the J-aggregation, the g-CDs illustrate special red-shift excitation-dependent PL emission in their concentrated aqueous solution. Meanwhile, as similar as the b-CDs, the electron transition from respective a and d levels between different nanoparticles can also occur and result in the strong visible-NIR absorption. Finally, the changed PL emission and UV–vis absorption without well-defined strong absorption bands for the r-CDs are considered from the possible interparticle J-aggregation. Due to the self-assembled J-aggregation, the r-CDs aqueous solution illustrates special red-shift excitation dependent PL emission. However, the spatial arrangements of adjacent nanoparticles are also restricted, leading to the electron transitions from the a to d levels unfavorable and ineffective compared with the g-CDs. In addition, the spontaneous J-aggregation can also explain the changed PL emission and UV–vis absorption of fresh o-CDs solution in different concentration, in which the J-aggregation of o-CDs directly results in the red-shift of excitation-dependent PL emission and variable UV–vis absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Promising applications of the assembled CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Driven by possible electrostatic interactions between the charge confined surfaces, the monodispersed CDs can self-assemble into nanoaggregate in any possible three-dimensional spatial arrangement, resulting in the complex aggregation states. The characteristic visible-NIR absorption originated from the electron transitions through the coupled energy levels in the CD-based nanoaggregates can be obtained by increased assembly degrees, endowing their various potential applications. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the obtained powder of b-CDs, gr-CDs g-CDs and r-CDs exhibit excellent stability for up to three months in the absence of air. The redispersed aqueous solution of b-CDs and g-CDs exhibit similar PL emission as their initial solution (Fig. 6b and c), implying the feasibility and repeatability to manipulate the spectral characters of CDs. Meanwhile, the increased absorption in the visible-NIR band enables CDs with the promising applications in different fields, such as photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) (Fig. 6d). As shown in Fig. 6e, the photothermal conversation under NIR light of 100 mg L−1 o-CDs, b-CDs, gr-CDs g-CDs and r-CDs are investigated. In these nanomaterials, only the b-CDs exhibit excellent photothermal conversion under 780 nm laser, in which the temperature of b-CDs aqueous dispersions increases by 27.7–70.7 °C in 10 min under 10 W cm−2 irradiation. When the concentration of b-CDs decreases from 100 mg L−1 to 25 mg L−1, the maximal solution temperature decreases from ∼70 °C to ∼50 °C (Fig. 6f). Moreover, the 100 mg L−1 b-CDs aqueous solution illustrates stable photothermal conversation efficiency of 12.86% (Figs. S10–S12). And the different concentration of b-CDs aqueous solution exhibit stable photothermal conversion in five cycles under 780 nm laser or after long expose time (Fig. 6f and S13). The enhanced visible-NIR absorption provides the promising application of b-CDs for PTT. In addition, the amount of free radical production of the b-CDs and g-CDs aqueous solution have been also tested with light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6g and h, the cytotoxins production of singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) can be detected under the 650 nm laser, indicating the photodynamic capability of b-CDs and g-CDs.

With the above characterizations, a possible transition model can be proposed to the photothermal and photodynamic process of b-CDs and g-CDs in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 6i, the interparticle electron transitions between the trap-state energy levels on the surfaces provide the NIR absorption and the H-aggregation of b-CDs enable the favorable photothermal conversation. Meanwhile, the N atoms with lone pair electrons in the CDs can promote electrons transition from single state to triplet state. Thus, part of the excited-state electrons can transfer to the ambient H2O and dissolved oxygen and in turn lead to the type I and type II photochemical ROS production of 1O2 and •OH. In contrast to the reported carbon nanomaterials, the supramolecular aggregation state and enhanced visible-NIR absorption attributed to the interparticle electron can provide more strategies to engineer the photophysical properties of CDs. In addition, the cytotoxicity of b-CDs has been further investigated with HeLa cells by the MTT assay. The results illustrate that nearly 100% viability can be obtained when the concentration of the b-CDs is up to 500 μg mL−1, indicating the excellent biocompatibility of CDs (Fig. S14).

In summary, self-assembled hybrid of CDs has been observed under different aging treatment and these aggregated CDs in aqueous solution possess the PL and absorption characters alteration of H-/J-aggregation. With the experimental characterization and theory calculation, the surface functional groups on the CDs are proposed to bring in polarization for the CDs through the ionization effect of water, resulting in the tendency to form interparticle or intraparticle H-/J-aggregates of CDs. Whereupon, the inhomogeneous surface-confined charges and groups are responsible for the assembly by possible electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds, which facilitate the H-aggregate and J-aggregate and give rise to strong visible-NIR absorption. This work presents a novel concept of supramolecular engineering in CDs, which can be used to understand the luminescence mechanism and simultaneously regulate the photophysical properties of CDs.

Cheng-Long Shen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Qing Lou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Kai-Kai Liu: Visualization, Investigation. Guang-Song Zheng: Visualization, Investigation. Run-Wei Song: Visualization, Investigation. Jin-Hao Zang: Visualization, Investigation. Lin Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Chong-Xin Shan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of this research by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12074348 and U2004168), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M682310 and 2022TQ0307), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (212300410078 and 222102310664).
